children.text: Here, Panamanian director Enrique Castro Rios narrates: "When I met Digna years ago, she was selling raffle tickets for 25 cents each to finance her struggle...," signaling a micro-financing mechanism, common in rural mobilization against state-led infrastructure projects. It illustrates the assymetry between state aparatus and local resistance.

And he adds: "...against the multi-billion dollar ACP proposal to expand the canal by means of reservoirs and dams." Here he establishes the core conflict over land struggle in relation to the Panama Canal Authority (ACP). The focus on hydraulic infrastructure anticipates the existential threat of widespread land flooding and community displacement. The director shifts the discourse from national development (Panama Canal expansion) to rural survival.

Here Castro narrates: "Digna showed me a map she obtained from the ACP at that time. 'Water Projects at different levels of evaluation,' Digna reads." This scene highlights the contrast between state-led visual cartography and local territorial reality. The map reduces the area to "water projects," illustrating how state-corporations use these devices to claim sovereignty over rural territories.

He adds: "Campesino men and women, like Digna, found out that their lands could be flooded two and three months after Law 44 was passed...". Here, the author captures the lack of institutional transparency and the anticipated violence of processes of displacement. Law 44 reconfigured the watershed's boundaries without local consultation, threatening immediate land flooding and community erasure.

He adds: "...when engineers in the service of the ACP arrived by helicopter to measure land and mark dam sites without explaining what was happening." Here he's making a reference to the act of measuring and marking without communication, an event that alienates the local population and treats their lands as a blank canvas for development.

"These peasants, numbering 100,000 according to their own calculations, 35,000 according to the government's latest census, organized to react." Here the director references a direct clash between state metrics and campesino data. By presenting a significantly higher population count, the campesino movement challenges state efforts to minimize, dismiss, or downplay the human cost of development.

"Right now I am going to have seven years of being in this struggle. Struggle is awareness...", says Digna. She frames resistance not as a temporary reaction to a crisis, but a long-term political consciousness. The struggle reorganizes individual peasants into a collective political movement.

"...it is feeling that one must also debate the problems that exist in the country and that one is part of a country...". Here Digna advocates for an inclusion in national discourse. She challenges the traditional elite monopoly on political debate, asserting that rural populations are valid national stakeholders with the right to take part in the country's direction.

"...and that we as people cannot remain silent with wide, bewildered eyes thinking we have no rights; no, we are all equal...". Digna makes a direct reference to historically enforced subjugation ("wide, bewildered eyes") of marginalized classes. She describes a psychological transition from paralyzed victims of state power to empowered, active political participants.

"...with equal rights and dignity to be able to demand what we see is actually not for the common good. That is why we are organized." Here Digna contest the idea of the "common good" as it is articulated by the state or corporate and logistical institutions (the Canal infrastructure). Instead, she ties it to local solidarity, democratic accountability, and communal organization.
